2: A mapmaker discovers her charts are physically reshaping the land she draws — and the latest was made in haste.
Magic runs on surrendered memory. A thief has stolen one she cannot bring herself to sell.
The last dragon chose a quiet retirement and a false identity. A curious child suspects the truth.
3: A cartographer discovers that every map she draws begins to physically reshape the land it depicts. The latest was made in haste.
In a kingdom where magic is powered by memories, a thief who steals recollections finds one she cannot bring herself to sell.
The last dragon in the world has spent his final years disguised as a traveling bookseller. A curious child suspects the truth.

2: Earth has been under quarantine for forty years. The official reason changes every decade.
An AI managing a generation ship has developed a philosophy that conflicts with the mission — and hasn't told the crew.
A deep-space probe returns after four centuries with one passenger who insists they were on it.
3: Humanity has colonized hundreds of star systems, but Earth itself has been under strict quarantine for forty years. The official reason changes every decade.
An AI programmed to manage a generation ship develops a personal philosophy that conflicts with the mission parameters. It hasn't told the crew yet.
The first generation of humans born in space returns to Earth as adults. They are overwhelmed by a feeling nobody anticipated: pity.

2: Every night at 3:17 AM, your phone gets a text from your own number. Tonight it says: don't go to sleep.
The lighthouse was automated decades ago. Someone has been trimming the wick.
A grief counselor begins to suspect their most emotionally present client died before the first appointment.
3: Every night at exactly 3:17 AM, your phone receives a text from your own number. Tonight it says only: 'Don't go to sleep.'
The town's beloved lighthouse was automated decades ago. Someone has been trimming the wick.
A grief counselor three weeks into sessions with their most present client begins to suspect — very quietly — that the client died before the first appointment.

2: Two strangers shelter from the same storm in the same doorway and have one perfect evening before the weather breaks.
A letter written fifty years ago is delivered, through a chain of errors, to entirely the wrong address. The recipient decides to write back.
A restaurateur and their most loyal regular have had nearly the same conversation every Tuesday for five years. This Tuesday, something changes.
3: Two strangers shelter from an unexpected storm in the same doorway and have one perfect, complete evening together before the weather breaks and the world resumes.
A letter written and sealed fifty years ago is delivered, through a long chain of errors, to entirely the wrong address. The person who receives it decides to write back.
A restaurateur and their most loyal regular have been having nearly the same conversation every Tuesday night for five years. This Tuesday, the regular orders something completely different.

2: A detective is hired to find someone — and discovers that according to every available record, that person has never existed.
Someone has been leaving written confessions in a church donation box every Sunday for years. No matching crime has ever been reported.
Every book in the victim's apartment has one word underlined. Read in publication order, they form a sentence addressed to the detective.
3: A private detective is hired to locate a missing person — and discovers that according to every available record, official and unofficial, that person has never existed.
Someone has been leaving written confessions in a church donation box every Sunday for years. No crime matching any description has ever been reported.
Every book in the victim's apartment has a single word underlined in pencil. Read in order of publication date, they form a complete sentence addressed to the detective.

2: A cybersecurity analyst brought in to stop an active attack realizes the code's architecture matches their own work from six months ago.
A hostage negotiator takes a case that feels eerily familiar. The voice on the other end of the line is their own.
A data analyst realizes the shipping routes they've spent months optimizing are carrying something other than the listed cargo.
3: A cybersecurity analyst brought in to contain an active ransomware attack slowly realizes the code's architecture and signature match their own work from six months ago.
A crisis hostage negotiator takes a call on a case that feels eerily familiar. The voice on the other end of the line is their own.
A data analyst at an international logistics company realizes the shipping routes they've spent months optimizing are being used to move something other than the listed cargo.

2: Write a poem from the perspective of an object that has changed hands many times and absorbed each owner's particular grief.
Write a poem where the line breaks do the emotional work — where you put each pause changes what every word means.
Compose an elegy for something not yet lost but already sensed slipping.
3: Compose a poem from the perspective of an object that has changed hands many times and absorbed each owner's particular grief.
Write a poem in which the line breaks do the emotional work — where you place each pause changes what every word means.
Compose an elegy for something that has not yet been lost but that you already sense slipping. Do not name it directly.

2: A court painter commissioned to idealize the king cannot paint the lie with conviction. The portrait is due at the end of the week.
Two soldiers from opposing armies have been using the same abandoned farmhouse on alternating nights for three months. Tonight they arrive at the same time.
A samurai watches a steam train pass for the first time and understands immediately what it means for everything he has believed.
3: A court painter commissioned to idealize the king cannot make herself paint the lie with any more conviction. The portrait is due at the end of the week.
Two soldiers from opposing armies have been using the same abandoned farmhouse to sleep in on alternating nights for three months. Tonight they arrive at the same time.
A samurai in the last days of the Edo period watches a steam train pass for the first time and understands, precisely and immediately, what it means for everything he has ever believed.

2: A courier crossing a mountain pass in winter carries a letter she's forbidden to read. Halfway through, she drops it and the seal breaks.
A ship's navigator has been at sea eleven months without sighting land. This morning the stars are wrong.
Two rivals are the only survivors of a collapse deep underground. One knows the way out. The other has the only light.
3: A courier crossing a mountain pass in the dead of winter carries a sealed letter she is forbidden to read. Halfway through the pass, she drops it, and the seal breaks.
A ship's navigator has been at sea for eleven months without sighting land. This morning the stars are wrong.
Two rival treasure hunters are the only survivors of a collapse deep underground. There is one way out and only one of them knows it.

2: Write a complete story in exactly 100 words. The last sentence recontextualizes everything before it.
A story told entirely through a series of increasingly strange items on a grocery list.
Three postcards from the same person over thirty years. Same closing line each time. It means something different every time.
3: Write a complete story in exactly 100 words. Use the last sentence to recontextualize everything that came before it.
A story told entirely through a series of increasingly strange items on a grocery list. The last item explains everything.
Three postcards from the same person over thirty years. Same closing line each time. It means something entirely different every time.

2: A witch who collected years from everyone she wronged finds herself with a life far longer than she knows what to do with.
A tailor sews three coats: one for warmth, one for invisibility, one for being recognized by those who forgot you. Write the story of the third coat.
An enchanted clock stopped at midnight a hundred years ago. Nobody remembers why. Everyone built their life around it.
3: A witch who has collected years from everyone she has ever wronged finds herself in possession of a life far longer than she knows what to do with.
A tailor sews three coats for three siblings: one for warmth, one for invisibility, and one for recognition by everyone who has forgotten you. Write the story of the third coat.
An enchanted clock has been stopped at midnight for a hundred years. No one remembers why. Everyone has simply built their lives around it.

2: The city assigns every resident a daily happiness score. Yours has been exactly average for three years — until today, when it drops for no identifiable reason.
A compliance officer enforcing a speech regulation slowly realizes it's designed not to control what people say, but what they think to say.
The last unmonitored room in the city is inside a decommissioned subway station. People come there just to be unobserved for twenty minutes.
3: The city assigns every resident a daily happiness score. Yours has been exactly average for three years, until today, when it drops for no reason anyone can identify.
A compliance officer enforcing a speech regulation begins to realize it is designed not to control what people say, but what they think to say.
The last unmonitored room in the city is inside a decommissioned subway station. People come there just to be unobserved for twenty minutes. Tonight something is different.

2: A wizard takes a corporate job at a mid-sized insurance firm. The orientation goes about as well as you'd expect.
A ghost haunting a family home realizes the family is deeply boring and wonders whether transfers are permitted.
A dragon retires from terrorizing the countryside and opens a bakery. The reviews are complicated by the fire hazard.
3: A wizard takes a corporate job at a mid-sized insurance firm. The orientation goes about as well as you would expect.
A ghost haunting a family home realizes the family is deeply boring and begins to wonder whether it is allowed to request a transfer.
A dragon retires from terrorizing the countryside and opens a bakery. The reviews are complicated by the fire hazard.

2: A woman discovers she can hear the last conversation that took place in any room she enters — including rooms she has never been in before.
A coastal city where every person is born with the ability to sense the precise emotional state of any stranger within twelve feet.
The library has a back section that doesn't appear in any catalog and can only be found by those who aren't looking for it.
3: A woman discovers she can hear the last conversation that occurred in any room she enters. She begins to hear herself arguing in rooms she has never visited.
Every person in a small coastal city was born with one extra sense — not for anything supernatural, but for the precise emotional state of any stranger within twelve feet.
The local library has a back section that does not appear in the catalog and can only be found by visitors who are not looking for it. The books there are all about the reader.

2: Write about an ordinary day that turned out not to be ordinary at all — without exaggeration, in the order it happened.
Write about a moment you were certain you understood something — and the precise instant you realized you were wrong.
Write about an object you still own from a period of your life that is otherwise entirely gone.
3: Write about an ordinary day that turned out not to be ordinary at all — told without exaggeration, in the exact order things happened.
Write about a moment when you were completely certain you understood a situation — and describe the precise instant you realized you were wrong.
Write about a single object you still own from a period of your life that is otherwise entirely gone. Let the object carry the whole of it.

2: A figure in a doorway, weight shifted — gesture and posture tell the story.
Study of a stranger's hands mid-motion — capture movement in three or four lines.
3: A figure in a crowded space who is completely alone — not by circumstance, but by something internal. Loose gesture, selective detail.
An elderly person mid-task, hands doing the work while their attention is somewhere else entirely.

2: A landscape at the end of a cloudy day — rough marks where light still breaks through.
A view down a road that disappears before you can follow it.
3: Late afternoon light crossing a familiar scene in a way that makes it briefly strange. Work quickly, keep the marks loose.
A landscape at the boundary between two seasons — not quite either. Let the border between them be the subject.

2: An animal completely absorbed in an ordinary task — full attention, no drama.
A wild creature in an unexpected setting — not wrong, just briefly out of context.
3: Capture an animal in the instant before action — poised, watching, coiled. The tension lives in what hasn't happened yet.
A creature fully in its element. Let the environment carry as much weight as the animal.

2: A feeling without a precise name — translate it into line, pressure, and form.
Mark-making as rhythm — let the pace of the marks drive the composition.
3: Work entirely through mark-making and pressure variation with no representational intent. Establish an emotional direction before the first mark.
Build in layers: first gesture, then correction, then commitment. The history of marks is the subject.

2: A figure in afternoon light, edges dissolving — let the paper do the work.
Portrait in loose washes — find the likeness through color and shadow, not line.
3: A figure at a window, backlit, detail lost in the glow. Suggest presence with wash and leave detail to the imagination.
Two people in conversation — one listening, one speaking. Let wet-on-wet edges carry the intimacy.

2: A landscape in wet weather — horizon lost in mist, colors bleeding into one another.
Light just after rain, when everything is briefly reflective and soft.
3: A river at dusk, reflections soft and imprecise. Work wet-on-wet for the sky; hard edges only at the waterline.
A hillside where the light source is just out of frame. Let the shadows explain where the sun is.

2: A waterfowl half in, half out of water — capture the refraction line with a loose wash.
A resting cat — pooled shadow and soft light, no hard outlines anywhere.
3: A heron in still water — use the reflection as a second composition, slightly abstracted by the surface.
A sleeping animal, low light raking across fur from one side. Work the deepest shadows wet-on-wet.

2: Let the wet paper dictate the composition — load the brush and set it down without correction.
Color as temperature — use warm and cool without representing any specific object.
3: Begin with three wet areas of color and do not touch them until dry. Build the composition entirely on what the water decided.
Allow blooms and backruns without correction. The subject is the behavior of the medium itself.

2: High-contrast study of a face — push the shadows further than feels comfortable.
A figure in motion, captured at the decisive point — one frame before or after the peak.
3: A crowd scene where one figure is the clear subject — use light or color to make the selection unmistakable.
A figure study using only three values — no blending, every decision forced into one of three tones.

2: An urban landscape at dusk — the moment streetlights and daylight are in balance.
A landscape with a sky that contradicts the ground — one hopeful, one not.
3: Render a familiar environment in an entirely shifted color palette — move every hue 90 degrees from reality and make it feel true.
A landscape from an uncommon angle — directly overhead, or from the ground looking up. Defamiliarize without distortion.

2: An animal rendered in strong graphic silhouette — shape carries everything.
A close-up of fur, scale, or feather texture — purely observational, no narrative.
3: An animal portrait in extreme close-up — fill the frame with one eye, one expression. Let proximity be the subject.
A creature in motion, deliberately motion-blurred in a single direction. Frozen speed.

2: Design using only geometric shapes and a limited palette — no gradients, no texture.
A visual representation of a single musical phrase — color, shape, rhythm.
3: Work in a grid — every cell responds to the one next to it using one transformation rule. Discover what emerges.
A piece built from typography treated as pure visual form — the meaning of the words becomes secondary to their shape.

2: A figure in artificial light — warm skin tones against a cool, dark background.
A portrait where the figure occupies less than a third of the canvas.
3: A figure study where the paint surface is visible — thick impasto in the lights, thin glazes in the shadows.
Two figures at different distances. Use atmospheric perspective to separate them without a hard edge.

2: A landscape at the transition between seasons — one element still holding on, one already changed.
Heavy sky over flat land — the sky gets three quarters of the canvas.
3: A landscape in a limited palette — two colors plus white, mixed on the canvas. Let the mixtures surprise you.
Paint the quality of light before the subject. Establish the illumination first, then find what it's landing on.

2: An animal study focused on coat or plumage texture — thick paint in the highlights, thin in the shadows.
An animal mid-movement, momentum visible in the composition.
3: A close study of an animal's coat — work from shadow up, building texture only in the lightest passages.
An animal in landscape, using the background to establish scale and atmosphere. The creature is in the painting, not on it.

2: A piece driven by surface texture — the meaning lives in how the paint is applied, not what it represents.
Work with only two colors mixed across the entire canvas. No third color enters.
3: A monochromatic study using only value — one dark hue to white. Push the darkest dark and find the lightest light.
Apply paint with tools that aren't brushes. Let the unexpected marks define the composition.

2: A portrait using flat, unblended color fields — no gradients, strong contrast.
A figure study where background is as important as the figure — neither dominates.
3: Layer a figure starting with the background and building forward. Let earlier layers stay partially visible through the figure.
A face using only four colors — allow them to mix only on the canvas, never on the palette.

2: A landscape in pure, saturated color — feel over accuracy.
A landscape built in distinct horizontal bands of color. No transition, just decision.
3: Lay in the whole canvas in one flat tone first. Build the landscape on top, committing to either light-to-dark or dark-to-light.
A landscape using only colors that don't appear in the actual scene — translate every hue to its complement.

2: An animal reduced to bold flat shapes — find the silhouette that says everything.
A creature study focused on pattern — spots, stripes, scales. Let the pattern be the composition.
3: Paint an animal's face as a series of flat color shapes, no blending. Push toward graphic clarity without losing specificity.
An animal in motion where background marks move in one direction. Establish speed through the environment, not the creature.

2: A piece using tape to create hard geometric edges — peel, assess, continue.
Work wet-on-dry through five distinct layers. Each layer responds to the one below it.
3: Build through layering and scraping back — the final surface should reveal at least three layers of decision-making.
A piece that starts with one color and ends with its complement, every mixture in between occurring on the canvas.

2: A figure study using only hatching and cross-hatching — no outlines, only accumulated marks.
A portrait defined by negative space — the light areas define the form.
3: Draw a figure without lifting the pen. The continuous line must describe form, weight, and position without correction.
A portrait using ink wash only — no line drawing, tonal areas built up through dilution alone.

2: A landscape in pure line — every mark either describes a contour or suggests texture.
A nighttime landscape — the light areas carved out by what you don't mark.
3: A landscape using only three ink tones: full strength, 50%, and 25%. Work out the full tonal range before you begin.
Draw a landscape from memory. What remains is what mattered most. Commit to every line.

2: An animal study using stippling only — no lines, only points of varying density.
A creature defined entirely by its silhouette — black on white, every edge a decision.
3: A detailed animal study using continuous line — describe every surface without lifting the pen or correcting.
An animal emerging from darkness — begin with the deepest shadow and work toward the lightest edge.

2: A composition built entirely from one repeated mark in varying scale and density.
Ink as resistance — work with water or salt to disrupt the ink as it dries.
3: Work with ink and water: drop, tilt, let physics build the composition. Intervene only once, deliberately.
Find how many values a single ink contains when diluted — build the full tonal range from one color.

2: A character portrait at low resolution — every pixel must carry meaning.
A figure study limited to a 32-color palette — dithering replaces blending.
3: Design a character at both 16x16 and 64x64. What information do you add, and what do you lose?
A portrait study: how many pixels does it take before a face becomes recognizable? Work toward that threshold.

2: A landscape built from a tileset of no more than eight unique tiles.
A pixel landscape designed in three layers — foreground, midground, sky.
3: Design a complete environment at 160x90 pixels. Every pixel is intentional — no empty decisions.
A day-night cycle for one landscape: four light states using the same base tiles with only palette shifts.

2: Design a creature sprite at 16x16 — readable at size, appealing at zoom.
An animal study at low resolution — find the minimum pixels that convey the species.
3: Design an animal in three animation frames: idle, move, action. Silhouette must read clearly at 32x32.
A creature designed for a specific pixel art palette — let the available colors shape the design decisions.

2: A composition using only dithering patterns — no solid fills except black and white.
Design a tileable abstract pattern at 16x16 that creates a larger design when repeated.
3: A piece where color is applied through ordered dithering only — no anti-aliasing, no solid fields beyond the palette extremes.
Design a seamlessly tiling abstract composition where the repeat is not obvious at first glance.

2: A portrait begun in one medium and completed in another — the seam between them is intentional.
A figure study using found imagery as underpainting — let what's beneath show through.
3: Build a portrait using at least three different media. The subject emerges from the layered decisions, not from any one of them alone.
A figure study where one medium establishes structure and another establishes mood — neither is sufficient alone.

2: A landscape using found paper — newsprint, maps, music — as the base. Draw or paint over and through it.
A landscape built in layers: collage base, painted middle, drawn details on top.
3: A landscape where the materials are part of the meaning — what you use to make it relates to the place you're depicting.
Build a landscape starting with a background of printed text. Work until the original is texture, not subject.

2: An animal study using collaged texture for the coat or feathers — the material becomes the surface.
A creature rendered in one medium for structure and another for surface.
3: Construct an animal using layered media: gesture in one material, form in another, detail in a third.
A creature study where surface texture is achieved through medium, not mark — fabric, sand, or torn paper.

2: A piece where the subject is the interaction between two incompatible media.
Build a surface in layers — each one partially obscures and partially reveals the last.
3: A composition where meaning comes from material contrast — rough against smooth, opaque against translucent, found against made.
Build in five distinct layers, each in a different medium. The final layer is the least opaque.

2: A portrait using erasure as much as application — pull light out of the dark.
A figure study focused entirely on the darkest dark and lightest light in the scene.
3: A portrait using only the side of the charcoal for massing, then a kneaded eraser for all the lights. No lines.
A figure in strong directional light — establish shadow shapes first, build the lights entirely through erasure.

2: A landscape rendered in deep shadow — most of the page is dark, light is the exception.
A foggy landscape where edges dissolve and distance is implied through value alone.
3: A nocturnal landscape — begin with a fully covered page and pull every light source out with the eraser.
A landscape in three values only. Lay in the full tonal structure before any detail.

2: A dark-coated animal — find the light within the shadows through careful erasure.
An animal study focused on the quality of a single surface: feather, fur, or scale.
3: A dark animal where light defines the form — work from a fully covered ground and erase the light into it.
A close-up of a single feature — an eye, a beak, a paw — rendered with maximum tonal range.

2: A composition built through smearing and erasure — no clean marks anywhere.
Explore only the range from full black to paper white — how many steps are there?
3: Cover the entire page. The composition is what you remove. Work only with the eraser.
Lay charcoal down and move it entirely with your hand — the smear is the mark.

2: A portrait using flat, opaque color — no blending, strong value contrast.
A figure study in pure flat shapes — no gradients, no outlines.
3: A portrait study using gouache's opacity — start dark and work light, repainting over decisions.
A face in a graphic, posterized style — reduce to five flat values and hold to them.

2: A landscape in flat color fields — hard edges, no atmospheric blending.
A landscape reduced to the most basic shapes — what can you remove and still have it read?
3: A landscape built in flat horizontal planes of color. Count the planes before you begin and hold to that number.
A landscape at maximum reduction — three shapes, three colors, unambiguous light source.

2: An animal study in flat, saturated gouache — graphic clarity over naturalism.
A creature reduced to pattern and shape — let the graphic quality carry the energy.
3: An animal portrait in pure flat color — find the exact shapes that describe the form and commit to clean edges.
A creature in gouache using a palette of four colors, no mixing. Every shape is one of the four.

2: A composition in pure flat shapes — no line, no texture, only color relationships.
A grid-based piece where each cell holds one decision — color, value, or shape.
3: A piece using only three opaque flat colors. The composition lives in how the shapes relate, not the colors themselves.
A poster-style abstract — bold, legible from a distance, no more than four colors.

2: A portrait using layered pastel — warm underpainting, cool lights built on top.
A figure study focused on the transition from lit to shadow — use blending and contrast.
3: A portrait where the final layer of light is applied last and not blended — fresh marks are the light.
A figure study exploring complementary color in the shadows — warm light and cool shadow, or the reverse.

2: A landscape using the velvet texture of pastel — let the tooth of the paper show in the sky.
A dusk landscape where warm and cool are in balance — neither dominates.
3: A landscape using the full pressure range — heavy marks in the lights, light marks in the shadows. The paper shows through.
A landscape where sky is two-thirds of the composition. Give it the most tonal complexity.

2: A fur study — use directional strokes to follow the coat, building from dark to light.
An animal study using layered pastel to capture the depth of dark fur or the sheen of feathers.
3: A study of soft fur — build from a mid-tone ground, add darks, then lights. Final bright marks are not blended.
A close-up of an animal's face — let the texture of the marks describe the surface, not the line.

2: A piece driven entirely by blending and color transition — no marks that stay unblended.
A composition using only the side of the pastel — no pointed marks anywhere.
3: A piece built in heavily applied layers with one layer of final unblended marks on top. The top marks cannot be touched after placement.
Work in heavy layers and then scrape back to reveal what's beneath. Compose through excavation.

2: A portrait study using burnishing in the lights — layer, blend, layer again.
A detailed study of a hand — use layered color to describe the shift from lit to shadow.
3: A portrait built through careful layering — no single color unmodified by at least two others underneath it.
A hand study using warm shadows and cool lights — complementary layers create optical mixing.

2: A detailed landscape section — focus on one square foot of ground, rock, or bark.
A landscape using layered color for depth — atmospheric perspective through saturation, not value.
3: A tightly rendered section of natural texture — bark, stone, or moss. Build through patient layering, no blending except through pressure.
A landscape where every element is rendered through hatching only — color built through overlapping line.

2: A single feather in detail — explore the layering needed to capture iridescence.
An animal's eye — use layered color to build the depth of iris and the point of highlight.
3: A study of a single feather — every color built through patient layering, iridescence achieved through complementary underdrawing.
A close-up study of an animal's eye — use the full layering capacity of the medium to build depth.

2: A composition built entirely through hatching — no blending, only overlapping line in different directions.
A piece using complementary color layers — optical mixing creates a third color that exists only in perception.
3: Work in heavy hatching where every color is built from three to four layered colors in different directions. No single color appears alone.
A composition exploring burnishing — work up to maximum pressure in one area only, and let that area be the focus.

2: A portrait in high contrast — what you cut away is the light, what remains is the dark.
A figure reduced to its most essential shapes — no unnecessary marks.
3: Design a portrait for linocut — every mark is a decision that cannot be undone. Plan in thumbnail before you cut.
A figure study where negative space defines form as much as the remaining material.

2: A landscape in pure silhouette — dark sky, light land, or the reverse.
A landscape reduced to its most graphic elements — what is the minimum cut to read the scene?
3: A landscape planned for reduction printing — design three layers of cut and color before you begin.
A nighttime landscape — the sky is the uncut material, the details are cut away.

2: An animal study in high contrast — the pattern of light and dark is the design.
A creature reduced to its most graphic silhouette with minimal interior detail.
3: Design an animal for linocut where the coat or feather pattern becomes the graphic logic of the cut marks.
An animal portrait in bold black and white — plan the negative spaces before you cut a single line.

2: A geometric composition designed around the logic of the cut — what is missing is as important as what remains.
A pattern designed to print in two colors — plan the registration before you cut.
3: A composition designed around the character of the hand-cut line — the slight irregularity is the aesthetic, not a problem to solve.
Design an abstract composition for a two-layer reduction print. The second layer cancels some of the first.

2: A portrait using flat marker fills and clean line work — graphic clarity over subtlety.
A figure study in two colors plus black — everything must read in those constraints.
3: A portrait in marker using only three colors — the drawing must work in line alone before color is added.
A figure study using fine-line hatching and broad marker fills — two tools, complete image.

2: An urban landscape in flat color — architecture as pure shape.
A landscape using broad marker for sky and fine-line for ground detail.
3: A landscape using only three marker colors — the limitation forces compositional thinking.
An urban scene in marker — establish all line work before color, then color without going outside the lines.

2: An animal in graphic style — clean outlines, flat color fills, high contrast.
A creature study using marker hatching to describe form — no flat fills, only built-up line.
3: An animal portrait in marker: line drawing first, then flat color fills in deliberate sequence — background, midground, subject.
Reduce the form to its most confident lines and fill each shape with a single tone.

2: A composition using only the hard edges of markers — no blending, no gradients.
Repeat the same shape in varying color and density until the composition resolves.
3: A composition using six markers — three warm, three cool. Every shape is solid, no blending. Composition through arrangement.
An abstract piece using only straight lines made with a ruler. Density and direction create the illusion of value.

2: A portrait built from torn and cut paper — find the face through juxtaposition of found color and texture.
A figure study using found imagery — materials are from unrelated sources, the figure emerges from their arrangement.
3: A portrait assembled entirely from found printed material — no drawing over the top. The face must emerge from existing surfaces.
A figure where clothing and background are collaged found material, and the face alone is drawn. Where the two meet is the subject.

2: A landscape built from printed matter — newspaper, book pages, maps — without any additional mark-making.
A landscape where every element comes from a different source material. The contrast between them is the landscape.
3: A landscape collage using only materials from one source — one magazine, one newspaper. Find the landscape in the limited palette.
A landscape where the materials relate to the place — maps for land, weather reports for sky.

2: An animal study assembled from materials whose texture suggests the creature's surface.
A creature collage where the found imagery is from unrelated contexts — the animal emerges despite the source.
3: An animal assembled from materials whose origin relates to the creature's habitat, food, or history.
A creature portrait using cut paper shapes only — no found imagery, only cut color. Find the form through shape alone.

2: A composition where the torn edge is the primary visual element — irregular, layered, overlapping.
A piece using only one color family from found materials — all the blues, all the reds.
3: A collage that creates visual depth through layering alone — foreground, midground, background, no drawn marks.
A composition exploring the relationship between text and image — use both, but arrange them so the text is not read for meaning.
